summary: In March of 1943, after I turned 18, I was drafted and sent to Fort Oglethorpe, Georgia for my physical examinations and induction into the Army. We stayed there about a week while they processed us. It was my first step into military life, marking the beginning of my service during World War II.
I lived in Memphis, Tennessee my entire life before joining the Army. When I was drafted in 1943, I was still living there. Memphis was my home and the place I came from before entering the military.

After induction, I was sent to Camp Butner in North Carolina for six months of hard training. It was there that I trained with the 277th Field Artillery Regiment using 105 mm Howitzers. The training was intense but prepared us well for combat.

My training took place in North Carolina, first at Camp Butner and then at Fort Bragg. At Fort Bragg, they switched our unit from the 105 mm Howitzer to the much larger 240 mm Howitzer. North Carolina was where I transitioned from basic training to handling the biggest artillery guns in the service.

I served in the 277th Field Artillery Regiment, initially using the 105 mm Howitzer during training at Camp Butner. Later, at Fort Bragg, we transitioned to the 240 mm Howitzer, the largest gun in the service. This regiment was my unit throughout the war, and I held multiple roles including Forward Observer and Cannoneer.

During my early training at Camp Butner, North Carolina, I operated the 105 mm Howitzer M2, which fired 60-pound shells. It was the first artillery piece I learned to use with my unit, the 277th Field Artillery Regiment. Later, we moved on to bigger guns, but the 105 mm was my introduction to artillery.

At Fort Bragg, North Carolina, my unit was switched to the 240 mm howitzer M1, which fired massive 360-pound shells. It was the biggest gun in the service, weighing 32 tons with a 30-foot barrel. Operating this gun was a major responsibility, and I was part of the crew that loaded and fired it during combat.

After training, we were shipped overseas to the European Theater of Operations during World War II. We left from New Rochelle, New York, aboard the Queen Elizabeth I and landed first in Glasgow, Scotland before moving to England. The ETO was where I saw combat, starting in France and advancing through Europe.

Our unit boarded the Queen Elizabeth I in New Rochelle, New York to head overseas. The trip was crowded, with 19,000 soldiers crammed on a ship built for fewer than 2,000. New Rochelle was the last American port we saw before crossing the Atlantic to join the fight in Europe.

Actually, we sailed on the Queen Elizabeth I, not the Queen Elizabeth 2, in 1944. The ship was incredibly fast, making the crossing in four and a half days, and it was packed with troops. It was a memorable voyage because of how crowded it was and how quickly we crossed the Atlantic.

Our first stop in Europe was Glasgow, Scotland, where we landed after crossing the Atlantic. From there, we took a train to Birmingham, England, and prepared for the invasion. Glasgow was our initial entry point into the European Theater of Operations.

After arriving in Glasgow, we traveled by train to Birmingham, England, where we bivouacked outside the city. We stayed there until the invasion of France began. Birmingham was part of our staging area before heading to the front lines.

Before the invasion, our unit conducted maneuvers in Wales, including Cardiff. It was part of our final preparations before heading to France. Wales offered a place to train and get ready for combat operations.

We conducted maneuvers in Cardiff, Wales, as part of our training before the Normandy invasion. It was a chance to practice and prepare for the upcoming combat in France. Cardiff was one of the places where we honed our artillery skills.

We landed in France on D-Day plus ten and immediately set up our artillery guns near St. Mere Eglise. We advanced through France, participating in battles and moving toward Paris. France was the main battleground where I first saw combat and where much of our fighting took place.

We landed in France shortly after D-Day, around the 12th or 13th of June 1944. Although we missed the initial invasion day, we were part of the follow-up forces that established artillery positions to support the advance. D-Day marked the beginning of our combat operations in Europe.

Our first combat artillery setup was near St. Mere Eglise, a small town in France. We landed nearby and quickly established our guns to support the front lines. St. Mere Eglise was significant as the place where I first experienced combat conditions.

We landed in France aboard a Landing Ship Tank, or LST, which carried our tanks and equipment to the shore. The tide had gone out, so we actually rode our tanks off onto the land. The LST was crucial for getting heavy equipment and troops safely onto the beaches.

After advancing through France, we reached the outskirts of Paris in September 1944. The entire Army was put on hold there for about a month, which felt like a vacation with good beds and showers. Paris marked a pause in our campaign before pushing further into Germany.

While advancing through France, I was wounded near Avranches and sent back to a hospital in England to recover. I rejoined my unit just before we entered Germany. Avranches was where I got hurt during the campaign.

We entered Germany after our time in France and experienced heavy fighting there, including being shelled by German 88 mm guns. I was caught outside during a shelling and witnessed casualties close up. Later, I returned to Germany in 1985 to visit the site of the Malmedy massacre where American soldiers were killed.

In Germany, we came under heavy fire from German 88 mm guns. I remember crouching beside a tank for about ten minutes while they shelled our position. The 88 mm was a deadly weapon that caused many casualties in our unit.

One of the most memorable and intense experiences was the Battle of the Bulge in December 1944. We were caught in the German breakthrough near St. Vith, Belgium, just 15 miles from Bastogne. The fighting was brutal, and we were ordered to destroy our guns at one point but then told to hold them. It was a harsh winter with freezing conditions that caused many injuries.

During the Battle of the Bulge, we were stationed in the town of St. Vith in Belgium. It was a critical location about 15 miles from Bastogne where heavy fighting occurred. We faced intense German attacks and harsh winter weather while trying to hold our position.

Bastogne was a key town near where we fought during the Battle of the Bulge. It was about 15 miles from St. Vith, where my unit was positioned. The German offensive aimed to capture this area, making it a focal point of the battle.

During the Battle of the Bulge, a German unit captured about 110 to 115 American soldiers and took them to Malmedy. In freezing weather, the Germans executed most of them in what became known as the Malmedy massacre. Years later, in 1985, my wife and I visited the memorial site in Germany where their names are inscribed on a circular stone wall.

Malmedy was the town in Belgium where the massacre of American prisoners took place during the Battle of the Bulge. The captured soldiers were taken there and killed by German troops in brutal winter conditions. The event left a lasting impression on me and my unit.

General George S. Patton commanded the Third United States Army, which my unit was part of during the European campaign. His leadership was well known, and we advanced under his command through France and into Germany. Patton's aggressive tactics shaped much of our combat experience.

I served under the Third United States Army commanded by General Patton. This army was responsible for many of the advances through France and into Germany. Being part of this unit meant we were often at the forefront of the fighting in the European Theater.
The transcript does not mention my involvement with the Seventh United States Army, so I have no direct experience or stories related to that unit during my service.
